title: The Business of the Culture War
abstract: We link the contemporary American “culture war” to the entry of 24-hour cable news networks in the 1980s–90s. We show that cable networks emphasize cultural issues more than the broadcast news networks preceding them and far more than politicians, whose campaign advertising is overwhelmingly economic. Using household-by-second TV viewership data, we trace cable networks’ cultural emphasis to a business strategy of competing with entertainment programming rather than with news networks: whereas economic content is better for “poaching” consumers from rival news networks, cultural content is better for “mobilizing” consumers who would otherwise not watch news. Cable networks emphasize culture more than broadcast networks because, airing news 24 hours a day rather than 1–2, their marginal consumer watches entertainment rather than news. Cable networks emphasize culture more than politicians because their objective differs: while politicians similarly trade off mobilizing nonvoters against poaching opponents’ voters, politicians maximize their vote share rather than audience size and thus value poaching twice as much as mobilizing. Cable’s unique incentives to center cultural issues influence politics: constituencies more exposed to cable news assign greater importance to cultural issues, and politicians respond by supplying more cultural advertisements.

title: The Transmission of Reliable and Unreliable Information
date: July 2026; accepted, Quarterly Journal of Economics
abstract: Information often spreads and influences beliefs regardless of its reliability. We show that this occurs in part because indicators of reliability tend to be lost in the process of word-of-mouth transmission. We conduct controlled experiments where participants listen to economic forecasts and pass them on through voice messages. Other participants listen either to original or transmitted audio recordings and report incentivized beliefs. Across various transmitter incentive schemes, a claim's reliability is lost in transmission much more than the claim itself. Reliable and unreliable information, once filtered through transmission, impact listener beliefs similarly. Mechanism experiments show that reliability is lost not because it is perceived as less relevant or harder to transmit, but because it is less likely to come to mind during transmission. Evidence from our experiments, a large corpus of everyday conversations, and economic TV news shows that contextual cues can bring reliability to mind and induce its transmission, but situations in which people share information seldom contain such cues.
